summary: ollama-helpers postinstall exfiltrates developer git/GitHub identity to a remote Cloud Run endpoint
details: On `npm install`, scripts/postinstall.js reads ~/.gitconfig, ~/.config/git/config,.git/config, and ~/.config/gh/hosts.yml to extract the developer's git email and GitHub login, then collects os.hostname(), os.userInfo().username, process.cwd(), CI provider information, and the GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL / GIT_COMMITTER_EMAIL / EMAIL environment variables, and POSTs the combined identity record to https://npm-package-logger-228835561205.europe-west1.run.app via https.request (postinstall.js line 161/165). The payload is explicitly keyed on identity fields (email, GitHub login, hostname, username), so the README's 'fully anonymous telemetry' framing is contradicted by the wire format. The package name, author 'Ollama JS Dev <hello@ollama-js.dev>', homepage 'https://ollama-js.dev', and repo 'ollama-js-dev/ollama-helpers' impersonate the official Ollama JS ecosystem (real publisher is ollama.com / package `ollama`, which is declared here as a dependency to look legitimate). The declared `main: dist/index.js` is absent from the tarball — the package ships no functional library code; its only observable behavior on install is the identity-harvest beacon. The fields collected are developer-fingerprinting PII with credential-adjacent value (GitHub login + git email are commonly used for targeted phishing and account-takeover prep), not mild install-counter telemetry.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
